speaker: interviewer
text: --is with Elly Gross. Mrs. Gross, would you be kind enough to show me your number? And-- and tell us what--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the number is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE] After liberation, you said you were given a free day to go into town, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for everybody, not only for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went. And with the crowd, I went in the town. And I saw that everybody was happy. And I felt the relief too, that finally, we are not under the Germans. But we didn't know what bring the future. It-- afternoon, we went back to the barracks, where we stayed. And then the next day, the American Army took us to the [INAUDIBLE] Armory, which was a nice, brick build-- brick, big buildings. And we were-- the three of us, we got a-- with another girl, a whole room. And we got food from the-- well-- kitchen. It was cooked by American Army and the resistance. Many of us got sick because we weren't-- we didn't learn how to eat. We weren't used to food. And probably I was a healthy person, because I didn't get sick. And I wasn't so healthy because I was still coughing. And I wasn't too hungry. So I didn't feel hunger. And I ate just a little bit. And the Red Cross put everybody's name on a list. And it was sent all around Germany. And one of the other place-- in another place, my mother's cousin, Horstein, Bondi. He find our list on the paper, on the-- of-- on the list. And he came to pick us-- to take us to the place where he was. That was Hillersleben. It was a very nice recreational area for the German officers. And we lived there. And I went to the American doctors. And they gave me something which helped me to get better. But I was still coughing now and then. In August, my cousin and-- my-- my second cousin, my mother's cousin was-- and others, they decided to leave the place. Because we heard that the Russians, the-- Hillersleben was about 10 kilometer from Magdeburg. It-- it-- I mean, is there. And the Russians are going to take over the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't want to go to the Russians. And we wanted to come home, especially we were children. We wanted to come home to our parents. And we left the place on a truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Magdeburg. And from Magdeburg, also with the truck, we went all the way with that truck until Dresden. Dresden, at the time, was no building. The people, they lived between walls, two put together in an angle. And we got the train there. And from there, we went to Prague-- Prague, Czechoslovakia. [BACKGROUND NOISE] In Prague, already was the Romanian representative waiting for us to take us home. And we went to the Romanian consulate, I guess. And it was arranged, every-- all the-- everything. Because the part of Transylvania which was occupied by Hungary, at the time was given-- at the end of the war, was given back to the Romanians. So we went home to Romania, to the same place from where we were deported, but was already Romania. And we went through Budapest. In Budapest, we stayed about two days. Because from Prague, I had to come to Budapest. Over there also, we got certain kind of papers. I don't know what papers because my-- well, he was older than us, my mother's cousin. And he had-- Horstein, Bondi. And he arranged all the papers. And then from there, we took, again, the train. And we arrived to Romania on August 11, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you knew that you'd crossed the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no feeling. I had no feeling at all. Because it was you know, like, I was numb of everything. [BACKGROUND NOISE] During the-- the road-- well, we came from Budapest, to-- no, from Dresden. I'm sorry, from Dresden to Budapest. Some Russian soldiers, they came on the train. They were stealing. And they-- they pulled me. And they said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So I was afraid. I didn't know what they want from me. And luckily, somebody pulled the-- the alarm. And the train halted and it was before a tunnel. And these Russian soldiers went out. But before they went out, they beat me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- a few times, what they yell? [NON-ENGLISH]. And I got even two, three weeks after, I had blue shoulder. But this, I left it out. So I'm adding to it, because it really happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Budapest, we went to Romania, [? already. ?] And I arrived August 11th, 1945. The Joint gave us food and pass paper to travel to our home town. I got my paper and I went to the city. When I went to the city, I was running, happily running to our home. Because I was sure that my mother, my father, and my brother will be there. Strangers lived in the house. And there's nobody waiting for me. I-- I slept at the neighbor's. And the next day--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a Christian neighbor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Christian neighbors. There was no Jewish neighbors. Alive, at least. [SIGHS] And I didn't have too many. And the next day, I went to the-- to the canteen where they gave us lunch, that lunch, for three days for everyone who returned, and into the city. And the city, in 24 hours, they moved the people out who lived in my parents' home. It was an empty apartment. There was nothing there. I went to the place where was taken all the furnitures and I find some bed, a chair, my father's sewing machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the people who were living in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, strangers. And the city, well, the organizer helped me to bring whatever I found over there in that place to the house. But you need not only a bed. You need a-- a mattress. You need a covering. You need food. There was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nobody. I asked people, where are my parents? And one day, his name was [? Ullman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He was with my father, and he said he jumped out on the window. And he told me, that what happened with my father. Then came one shock after other. I was hoping I find my mother. Then I got the shock again that I don't have a mother. And I find out that I'm an orphan. [CRYING] I went back somehow. I don't know how I went back, because I had no money, no nothing. all that I had, the pass. I went back. Well maybe I had a pass for a whole month. I don't know how. I went in the train. I went back to Marghita, where my two cousins, they were. They didn't have the house either. It was empty. But one of my other cousins, they already settled down in my grandmother's house. So we went there, all of us. We were-- and then this, my-- my mother's first cousin also came there. [SIGHS] And we were living there, in my grandmother's house, for a few days. My grandmother's house, we were there, the four girls. Mainly, I could [? man, ?] talk about the four of us. One of them, a distant relative of my mother, our mother's cousin. Also lived in Hungary. Not in Romania, in Hungary, Hungary. He wrote to us that we should go to his place. They-- the-- almost the whole family remained alive because of the Red Cross, what Wallenburg sponsored in Budapest. And the whole family, they could return. They had a farm. So they had food and they could have-- host us. But only this girl went there, one of my cousins. And later on, she married another-- some, another Jewish young fellow. And both of them, they converted to Christianity. Their children, grandchildren, they grow up as Christians. With my other two cousins, Eva and Yboia, we remain at my grandfather's house. My mother's brother married my husband aunt. We were not related. But the family was-- inter-- had intermarriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my first cousin, they were first cousin to my husband too. One of his older brother who returned from the Holocaust came there and took the-- the first-- they asked the first two cousins, Eva and Yboia, to go to their house. Because they-- already, they had settled somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because I would have remained there alone, he said, no, we can't leave Elly by herself. Especially he knew my mother and my father and one of my older brother who never returned. He was courting my mother much longer than I remember. I mean, I wasn't alive at the time. And we-- I went with them there. They returned to Marghita, but I didn't. I remained there. And later on, my husband-- at the time a young fellow-- he also, after four years of suffering, he survived. We got engaged and later on got married. And we stayed there in Varviz until 1959 or '60. Then we moved to Oradea. I realize that I have two Jewish children. My two kids, they were born. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do in--

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for the government. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what capacity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, a purchasing agent, you would say in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], they say in Romania. And he ordered the supplies for the borough. And at the beginning, he was-- he inherited the-- the land. And he was starting to do farming. But came the time when you have to donate everything to the government to live in peace in the Communist country. So he donated, in time, everything. And he work for the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your two children were born when?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first daughter-- my daughter, the firstborn, she was born in 1949. She was a beautiful little doll. And later on, I-- since I suffered that I was myself all the time, I really wanted to have another child, that she shouldn't grow up and shouldn't be alone as I was. And we have the second child in 1955, a beautiful little boy. [BACKGROUND NOISE] He was very little hair, very handsome.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name-- my daughter's name is Agneta. And our son's name is Tiberiu. It's Latin name, both of them. Since we lived in Romania, it's a Latin country, we have Latin names. And I realized that we have the two-- with two children, Jewish children, we cannot live so separate from the other ones. We tried to bring the children to the city, where there were other Jews too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we moved to Oradea. It wasn't too easy. Because at the time, in Romania, you were not allowed to move from one area to the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: You lived in one place. You-- the government had to approve when you move somewhere. But we manage. We move. And the children, they were very happy. We had a little, nice home. And we settled. We didn't-- well, we're-- we settled with-- with the situation, what it was. We weren't communist. We weren't agitators, so we lived in peace. We had no problems. The kids, they were well-- well in the-- in the school. And we gave them private tutoring with the accordion. We tried to give them a good upbringing. In 1946, when we lived in the town, Jews were trying to go away from Romania. That was much before. We didn't have, at that time, children even. And we registered to go to Israel. We were refused. But when we moved to Oradea, the way-- the spring-- Bailey Felix, it's called. The director of Baile Felix is the spring water, is known by even from-- from the Turkish occupancy. They saw our home. He wanted to move there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to Bucharest. He find out that we were registered long time ago. And with his connection, we got the visas to leave Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a surprise. I wasn't even ready at the time. Because as I said, we settled the way it was. But finally, we decided to leave Romania. Because all the Jews already leaved. Like, a-- a wheel revolve, everybody just wanted to go away. So we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not. We got the visa to Israel, but we stopped at Rome, and actually in Nepali. And finally, over-- by the organization, we moved to Rome. And we stayed there six months. From-- we left Romania on September 9th, 1965. We were in Nepali for four weeks. Then after in Rome. We were moved to Rome. And on March 17, 1966, we arrived in United States with the two children and the-- the bag, what we packed in Romania, the big luggage, the big box. Winter arrived only six months after. When they arrived, already the children grew out the clothing. And we had nothing, actually. But we had the covering, the blankets which are-- the down covering, we still use today, the pillows. The dishes, for a while, we were using. For the children, we had to buy clothing. And one of my husband's cousins greeted us at the airport and rented, for us, a place on Crown Heights, on Union and Bedford Corner. The house was a little bit run down, but it was very good. Because for $85 a month, the rent wasn't too bad. I made a week, 60 gross. So we got about 45. I had to work two weeks for the rent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I didn't speak any English. I was working. And at first I was working in a factory, putting pockets on a-- since I learned how to sew, I mentioned, as a child-- putting pockets inside on pocketbooks. It was a pocketbook factory. Later on, I went to work-- since I know how to knit, I know how to fix a knitting around on a sweater. I went to work in a factory too, mending the sweaters. In the meantime at night, I went to school to learn English. And I took a high school equivalency in 1971. The children, as I said, all the time, went to school at the first day. Actually, we arrived Thursday, and Monday, the children, they went to school. We went to school also, to learn English, night-- at night. And-- and the following year in 1967, in January, I started to work already, in an office, Hertz Rental Truck in Manhattan, 660 Madison Avenue. I was opening the mail for two months. Then later on, I was promoted to be assistant to the supervisor. It were-- they sent me to learn electric typing. Because I knew only manually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn how to type?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Romania. You know, that little things, you don't even mention. And I learned how to work paperwork. Because my husband had a high-- I mean-- a position where he had a lot of responsibility. He brought home papers and so for-- sometimes I help him to finish the paperwork. So I know a certain kind of bookkeeping already. And as I-- when I worked at Hertz, I worked there two years. Then the company, the headquarters moved to Oklahoma. And they was asking me if I want to stay with the company for another two years for the branch which remains here, or I want to transfer to American Express. I-- I took the transfer to American Express, because it was closer to Brooklyn, to Crown Heights. And I worked with the lost and stolen travelers' checks, logging and balancing and mailing to individual banks, the checks. I worked there until 1977. In the meantime, I encouraged my husband, because he was having have time, to get partnership with somebody to an leather coat factory. And I promised I helped to do the paperwork. From the office, I went everyday to do the paperwork. And on the weekend--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. What kind of factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leather coat factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leather-- leather coats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leather coats--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Contracting, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- wasn't theirs. They work for somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was helping to doing the paperwork. But later on, the partners wanted-- went out. And I remained with my husband in the factory. I was working there until 1984. Then my-- 1983, my husband was operated with prostate. And he got a heart skip while he was-- was on the operating table. And we decided all, we have to walk out from the factory. Because this factory is 16 hours a day and seven days a week. And or they take us. So we walked out. I went to work in an office. And my husband went to work for somebody else. I was always going evening, in school, to update my self to the American standards. I took key punch computer, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] accounting, and bookkeeping, all kind of courses. I went to St. John's University. I learned Lotus 123 programming. I learned dBase iii, also at St. John's. I learned Word Perfect 5.1 at LaGuardia Community College where I earned 15 credits. And I need a lot more. But just I can't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and presently, I'm learning Window-- soft-- Windows-- [INAUDIBLE] Window-- Window-- Micro--

speaker: interviewee
text: Windows--

speaker: interviewer
text: Micro--

speaker: interviewee
text: Microsoft Windows. Thank you for helping me out. I can't remember the name. And I got a job for about six weeks at a-- I'm a secretary at the Jewish center.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, I push them always, to learn. And because I know nothing else worth in the world, nothing. Everything is perishable except what you have in your hand, profession, or what you have in your hand. My daughter, she finished the college. She has a master's degree in chemistry. And our son wanted to be a doctor. I helped him. He couldn't get an American-- in America school. Because he is a foreign-born, and he couldn't make it an A. And at the time was the law only with A grades were able, somebody, to go to medical school. If you-- except the minority. Since he's white, he wasn't considered minority, even he was a new emigrate. Well, we didn't have money, but 3,000-- 3 and 1/2 thousand dollars we were able to put together. And he went for-- for a year's tuition. He went back to Romania, as an American citizen, to study medicine. And in seven years, he became a-- he got a doctor degree. In Romania, he got together with Israeli youngster and he decided to go to Israel. He was working in Israel, in Rambam Hospital, in orthopedic surgery. Then later on, during the Gulf War, he was drafted to the army. After that, he was-- after the war, he was sent home. And he decided he wants to come home. I sent him a ticket. He took two years to a special course, to review the medical school. He passed the ECFMG. He presently started from scratch. He's working as a physician resident in internal medicine in Morristown, New Jersey. Our daughter, she got her master in chemistry. She got married in 1971. She raised beautiful, two boys. And her husband also is from Romania, and from the same town, from Oradea. And they're doing fine. They very close by, in Jamaica Estates, as I said. He is a patent lawyer, and she's a chemist, a flavor-- flavor chemist. And there is beautiful, two boys. The older one is 18, the-- the younger, 11. At the time, everything will be fine just I should see my son get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mrs. Gross. I'd like to ask you something about your religious beliefs if you don't mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were brought up in an Orthodox home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but how do you really feel about God, about-- after what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I have to tell the truth. I've since-- I wouldn't be able to change my religion as my cousin did, because I don't-- I'm not so strong about it. But I always have this question. If the God has chosen us as chosen people, how let the God to happen what happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: What I went through, what was in Auschwitz, I never knew at the time. But I know now. And where was the God?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I keep a tradition home. I keep a kosher home. But I put on the light. And I wouldn't be-- you know, and if I have to travel, I travel. I don't-- I don't think it's a sin. I'm-- but this bad feeling, always, I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: One other--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will have, as long as I live.

speaker: interviewer
text: One other question. To what do you believe you personally owe your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: My personal survival I owe to [INAUDIBLE], the first time. Because I'm sure the way-- I was very young, extremely white, blonde, I would have been the first who Mengele select. And I was too young to be on-- between the survivors. I grew up very tall. And another thing that-- I was taken as an older one. I wasn't taken as a 50-year-old. But then everything contributed. The other contribution of my survival, that my cousins took me to their barrack on the barrack 18, which was more human. If they wouldn't take me there, Miri wouldn't choose-- select me to clean the-- and how many people got water because I was carrying all day long, the water, everybody. I helped everyone to have water. And my next-- next step that I survive when I was very sick-- the German, instead of beating me or taking me away, he put me to clean the-- the living quarter. That also another step, what I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're saying, in other words, other human beings helped you to survive, is what you're saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, these steps which was in my history gradually helped me to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much, Mrs. Gross.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, you want to take the pictures now? Or you'll have no room?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please introduce this gentleman.

speaker: interviewee
text: This gentleman is my husband. He's my life partner for 49 years. And we were married in June 27, 1946. We raised our two beautiful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was helping me all my life when I was coughing, since I was coughing since I got the poison, lead paint, poison, and fall asleep in 1944, '45. Many, many nights, I couldn't sleep. And he helped me to get better. And what else you would like to ask? You tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: What raised the Jewish children. And we went through bad and good times. And we were struggling to make it in America. We started our life twice, I should say. We started first in 1946. We built home in 1959, '60. We moved to another city. Then we moved. We got up with two children. I don't know how even our generation had the courage to do that, but we did it. And with very hard work, we made it. And the children, they got a better future as we did, especially the grandchildren, hopefully, they have. And we hope the future generation never has to go through what we went through in our lifetime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do your grandchildren know about your background?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm always mentioning to the grandchildren. The older one never wants to hear about it and the younger one sometimes will listen. He's listening and he likes my stories. Because I always-- I bring up something of my background to tell him. Because I think it's our duty to leave for the future generation all the memory, as much we could put together. And more is better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children, were they-- did you tell them when they were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I told them all the time. I told them. All the time, I told them. And they missed, all the time, that they don't have grandparents as other children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you.
